mcqs.question: What is the fundamental assumption of density-based clustering methods like DBSCAN?

options.text: Clusters are spherical and equally sized
Clusters are dense regions separated by sparse regions
The number of clusters must be known beforehand
All data points must belong to some cluster

mcqs.explanation: Density-based methods operate on the core principle that clusters are dense regions of data points in the feature space, separated by regions of lower density (noise). This contrasts with centroid-based methods that assume spherical clusters.

mcqs.question: In DBSCAN, a point is classified as a 'Core Point' if:

options.text: It is the centroid of a cluster
It has at least MinPts points within its ε-neighborhood (including itself)
It is the farthest point from any other cluster
It lies on the boundary between two dense regions

mcqs.explanation: A core point is the foundation of a cluster in DBSCAN. It must have a minimum number of neighboring points (MinPts) within a specified distance (ε). This density condition defines the seed from which a cluster expands.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a key advantage of DBSCAN over K-Means?

options.text: Requires specifying the number of clusters (K)
Can only find spherical clusters
Automatically identifies noise/outliers
Is more computationally efficient for large datasets

mcqs.explanation: DBSCAN explicitly models noise as points that do not belong to any dense region. This is a major advantage as it makes the algorithm robust to outliers, unlike K-Means which forces every point into a cluster.

mcqs.question: What is the role of the ε (epsilon) parameter in DBSCAN?

options.text: It defines the maximum number of clusters to form
It specifies the radius of the neighborhood around a point
It is the learning rate for the algorithm
It determines the convergence threshold

mcqs.explanation: Epsilon (ε) is the radius of the neighborhood circle around each data point. It is used to find the neighboring points and assess the local density. Choosing ε is critical: too small finds nothing as dense, too large merges everything.

mcqs.question: A point that is within the ε-neighborhood of a core point but is not itself a core point is called a:

options.text: Noise Point
Seed Point
Border Point
Centroid Point

mcqs.explanation: A border point is reachable from a core point (lies within its ε-neighborhood) but does not satisfy the MinPts condition to be a core point itself. It lies on the fringe of a dense region.
